title: Souvlaki, chicken
subtitle: Yoghurt-marinated chicken skewers with lemon and oregano
excerpt: Boneless chicken thighs marinated in Greek yoghurt, lemon, oregano, and garlic, then grilled on skewers until charred and cooked through. The yoghurt marinade keeps the chicken moist and gives the exterior a slightly caramelised crust. Served in pitta with tzatziki and tomato.
sourceNotes: Chicken souvlaki is widespread in Greece and has largely overtaken lamb in the tourist economy, though pork remains the dominant meat in traditional souvlaki shops. Yoghurt marinades for grilled chicken are documented across the Aegean and eastern Mediterranean, and are related to the shish taouk marinades of Lebanese cooking.

content.text: The yoghurt does two things in this marinade. The acid pulls the flavourings into the meat. The protein coats the surface and shields it from direct heat, so the outside chars without burning. You end up with a chicken skewer that crisps at the edges and stays juicy in the middle, the same effect as a good döner or taouk.

Use boneless chicken thighs rather than breast: the fat content means they stay moist over the high, direct heat of a grill. Breast pieces will be dry by the time they're cooked through. Cut into large pieces (4 cm) and don't crowd the skewer; the pieces need contact with the heat, not each other.

Combine the {{greek-yoghurt}}, {{lemon}} juice and zest, {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}}, {{garlic}}, {{oregano-dried}}, {{paprika-sweet}}, salt, and pepper in a bowl. Add the {{chicken-thighs-boneless}} pieces and turn to coat. Cover and refrigerate for at least 2 hours, or up to 24 hours.

Thread the chicken onto skewers, shaking off excess marinade. Heat a griddle pan, grill, or barbecue to high. Grill for 10 to 12 minutes, turning every 3 minutes, until charred in patches and cooked through, the juices should run clear when the thickest piece is pierced. Rest for 5 minutes.

Warm the {{pitta-bread}} directly on the grill. Slide the chicken off the skewers into the pitta and top with {{tomato}}, {{red-onion}}, and {{cucumber}}. Add a squeeze of lemon and serve immediately.

items.fix: Shake off the excess marinade before threading. Drop the heat after the first char. Or move to indirect heat to finish.
items.cause: Yoghurt and lemon contain sugars that burn quickly at high heat
items.symptom: Marinade is burning before the chicken is cooked

items.fix: Use thighs only; the correct internal temperature for chicken is 74°C, remove from the heat and rest, it continues cooking
items.cause: Breast pieces used, or chicken overcooked
items.symptom: Chicken is dry in the centre

items.fix: Heat until smoking before adding the skewers; brush the grill bars with oil just before placing the chicken
items.cause: Griddle not hot enough, or not oiled

items.fix: A thin coating of marinade chars and colours; a thick puddle steams the chicken instead
items.cause: Excess marinade not shaken off, or heat too low
items.symptom: Marinade has not coloured the exterior

content.text: Chicken souvlaki is the everyday version for home grilling in Greece and abroad. Chicken is cheaper than lamb and easier to cook well than pork shoulder. The yoghurt marinade brings it close to Lebanese shish taouk, a nod to the shared grill traditions across the eastern Mediterranean. In a pitta with tomato, onion, and tzatziki, it is fast summer food. The kind of dish that belongs outdoors in the early evening.
